title: Namibia Blocks Starlink Due to Strict Local Ownership Laws
description: Namibia has officially rejected Starlink's licensing application because Elon Musk's subsidiary lacks the required 51% local ownership.
lede: Elon Musk's Starlink has been denied a license to operate in Namibia, marking the satellite internet provider's latest regulatory roadblock in southern Africa. The nation's telecom regulator rejected the application because the company failed to meet the strict local ownership requirements.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: The Communications Regulatory Authority of Namibia (Cran) has officially rejected an application from Starlink to provide satellite internet services within its borders. While Cran did not detail all the reasons for the refusal, it clearly stated that Starlink's Namibian subsidiary failed to comply with local ownership mandates.

Under Namibian law, any telecommunications company operating in the country must have at least 51% of its shares owned by citizens or local entities. Starlink currently operates in approximately 25 other African countries but has hit a wall in nations with stringent domestic ownership rules.

This is not the first clash between the regulator and the satellite provider. In 2024, Cran issued a cease-and-desist order against Starlink for operating without a license and warned the public that purchasing its equipment or subscribing to the service was illegal.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: The rejection represents a significant setback for Starlink's expansion across the African continent. It echoes the company's struggles in neighboring South Africa, where it has also been blocked from entry due to local ownership policies.

Elon Musk has previously lashed out at these regulations. Last year, the billionaire claimed his service was barred from South Africa simply because he is not black, calling the nation's economic empowerment policies "racist ownership laws." The South African government dismissed these claims, noting that over 600 US companies successfully operate in compliance with their regulations.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: Namibia's strict ownership laws are rooted in its history. The nation was governed by South Africa's white-minority regime until gaining independence in 1990. Following independence, Namibia enacted policies specifically designed to increase local business ownership and correct historical racial inequalities.

Similarly, South Africa introduced black economic empowerment policies after white-minority rule ended in 1994. Those regulations require foreign investors to give local black-owned firms a 30% stake in their businesses, a threshold Starlink has thus far refused to meet.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: Starlink has not yet publicly commented on the latest regulatory rejection from Namibia. However, the company states on its website that it has formed a local entity to partner with Namibian firms and generate employment.

The door is not permanently closed. Cran has indicated that it could reconsider its decision either on its own motion or through a petition filed within 90 days, giving Starlink a brief window to restructure its local subsidiary to meet the 51% ownership threshold.
